1: astēra ek tou ouranou peptōkota eis tēn gēn — "a star that had fallen from heaven to earth." The perfect participle peptōkota (already fallen, in a state of having fallen) distinguishes this from the falling of Revelation 8:10 (aorist, the moment of falling). This star is already in its fallen state before John sees it — a being of previously higher status now consigned to the lower realm. The key to the shaft of the Abyss is given to him: the fallen one is granted authority over the prison-realm, not as a possession but as a temporary grant.
2: ho phreatos tēs abyssou — "the shaft of the Abyss" (literally: the well/pit of the bottomless pit). Abyssos in LXX translates tehōm, the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2 — the undifferentiated chaos before creation. In Jewish apocalyptic it becomes the prison of rebellious spirits (1 Enoch Revelation 18:11-16; Jude 6: "chains of darkness"). The smoke rising from the opened Abyss darkens the sun — the opened chaos-realm corrupts the created order from below.
3: akrides (locusts) — in the OT, the locust-plague is a recurring image of divine judgment (Exodus 10:12-15; Joel 1-2; Amos 7:1). Joel 1:6 describes the locust-army with lion's teeth, and Joel 2:4-5 describes them as horses rushing into battle — language John will reprise in vv7-9. The scorpion-power grants them the ability to inflict pain without killing — a new capacity beyond the historical locust's grain-destruction.
4: mē adikēsōsin — "they were commanded not to harm" (aorist subjunctive of prohibition). The specific exclusions (grass, green plants, trees) invert the normal locust pattern — the historical locusts destroy vegetation, these locusts target only unprotected people. The inversion signals that this is not literal entomology but a symbolic description of a spiritual/historical force. sphragida tou Theou (the seal of God) — the protection established in Revelation 7 holds: those sealed by God are exempt from the Abyss-locusts.
5: basanismos ... hōs basanismos skorpiou — "torment ... like the torment of a scorpion." The scorpion sting produces intense pain but rarely death; the five months of torment without death is a judgment of prolonged suffering. mēnas pente (five months) — the natural lifespan of a locust (May to September, approximately five months); the duration is calibrated to the creature's biology but the function is symbolic.
6: zētēsousin ... thanaton kai ou mē heurōsin — "they will seek death and will by no means find it" (ou mē + future is the strongest Greek negation). The reversal of death's availability — normally an ever-present option — is itself a form of judgment. Compare Job 3:21 (those who long for death that does not come) and Jeremiah 8:3 (death preferred to life). The inability to die transforms what might be merciful release into perpetual torment.
7: hōs hippoi hētoimasmenoi eis polemon — "like horses prepared for battle." The extended locust-description in vv7-10 is a series of simile-stacks (hōs, like): crowns like gold, faces like humans, hair like women's, teeth like lions'. John accumulates similes to describe what cannot be directly named — a composite creature from the Abyss that defies normal taxonomic categories. The horse-armor (Joel 2:4-5) is the base, elaborated beyond anything in the OT source.
8: trichas hōs trichas gynaikōn — "hair like women's hair." This detail is not in Joel's locust-army; it is John's addition. The significance is debated: some suggest the long, flowing mane of horses; others see it as an additional element of the uncanny, making the locust-creatures both masculine (battle-ready) and feminine (long hair) — a gender-crossing that signals their category-transgressing nature from the Abyss. odonas hōs leontos — Joel 1:6 explicitly gives the locust "teeth of a lion."
9: phōnē tōn pterygōn autōn hōs phōnē harmatōn hippōn pollōn trechontōn eis polemon — "the sound of their wings was like the sound of chariots, of many horses rushing into battle." Joel 2:5 ("like the noise of chariots they leap over the mountaintops, like the crackling of fire consuming stubble, like a powerful army drawn up for battle"). John's auditory simile draws directly from Joel's battle-army of locusts, but the addition of chariot-noise multiplies the scale.
10: hai ourai autōn homoiai skorpiois — "their tails were like scorpions." The double-ended threat (mouths that wound, tails that sting) makes the creature dangerous in all directions. to adikēsai tous anthrōpous mēnas pente — "to harm people for five months": the same duration restated, now attributed specifically to the tails. The repetition of the five-month limit emphasizes the constraint placed on even demonic power — judgment operates within defined limits.
11: Abaddōn ... Apollyōn — the Hebrew name given first (Abaddon, from abad = to perish/be destroyed; appearing in Job 26:6; Revelation 28:22; Revelation 31:12; Psalms 88:11; Proverbs 15:11 as the realm of destruction beneath Sheol), then the Greek equivalent (Apollyon, the Destroyer). The naming of the king of the Abyss with this word gives a personal identity to the realm of destruction — Abaddon is no longer just a place but a person/power. The Greek name Apollyōn also phonetically evokes Apollo, the Greek sun-god whose symbol was the locust; some scholars read an anti-imperial polemic against the Neronian or Domitian cult here.
12: hē ouai hē mia apēlthen — "the first woe has passed" — the perfect tense of departure (apēlthen, aorist actually, indicating completed departure). The structural marker divides the trumpet sequence and prepares for the sixth trumpet's even greater judgment. The announcement that "two more woes are still coming" creates narrative tension and eschatological urgency.
13: phōnēn mian ek tōn tessarōn keratōn tou thysiast ēriou tou chrysou — "a voice from the four horns of the golden altar." The horns of the altar were the points to which the blood of the sin offering was applied (Leviticus 4:7,18,25,30; Exodus 29:12); they were also grasped by those seeking sanctuary (1 Kings 1:50-51; Revelation 2:28). The voice from the altar-horns links the judgment of the sixth trumpet to the altar-prayers of Revelation 8:3-5 and the martyr-souls of Revelation 6:9-10: justice proceeds from the place of sacrifice and prayer.
14: luson tous tessaras angelous tous dedemenous epi tō potamō tō megalō Euphratē — "release the four angels bound at the great river Euphrates." The Euphrates is the eastern boundary of the promised land (Genesis 15:18) and the symbolic boundary between the covenant people and the threatening empires of Assyria and Babylon. The four bound angels (bound = dedemenous, perfect passive participle, in a lasting state of restraint) are released at the specific moment designated — the precise timing of v15.
15: hoi hētoimasmenoi eis tēn hōran kai hēmeran kai mēna kai eniauton — "prepared for the hour, day, month, and year." The fourfold time-specification (hour, day, month, year) indicates absolute divine precision: this judgment was ordained for this exact moment in the cosmic calendar. Hētoimasmenoi (perfect passive participle: having been prepared) — the preparation was completed in the past; the angels have been waiting in their bound state for this moment to arrive.
16: dyo myriades myriadōn — "two myriads of myriads" = 200,000,000 (two hundred million). Myrias is ten thousand, the largest numeral in Greek; myriad of myriads is the limit of numerical expression. The army is beyond counting-in-practice, echoing the innumerable host of 5:11 but now for judgment rather than worship. ēkousa ton arithmon autōn — "I heard their number" (not counted it) — John receives the numerical revelation aurally.
17: houtōs eidon tous hippous en tē horasei kai tous kathēmenous ep' autōn — "this is how I saw the horses in my vision." The vision-qualifier (en tē horasei, in the vision) maintains the visionary frame around the description. purinos kai hyakinthinos kai theiōdeis — fire-colored, dark blue (hyakinthinos, the color of the hyacinth flower, or possibly smoke-dark), and sulfur-yellow: the three colors of fire, smoke, and sulfur that are named in v18 as the three plagues. The horses' appearance pre-figures the judgment they carry.
18: ek tōn triōn plēgōn toutōn apektanthē to triton tōn anthrōpōn — "by these three plagues a third of humanity was killed." The three plagues (fire, smoke, sulfur — pyr, kapnos, theion) are also the substances associated with the destruction of Sodom (Genesis 19:24,28: fire and sulfur; smoke rising like smoke from a furnace). The echo of Sodom at the cosmic scale is deliberate: the sixth-trumpet judgment is Sodom writ large.
19: hē gar exousia tōn hippōn en tō stomati autōn estin kai en tais ourais autōn — "the power of the horses is in their mouths and in their tails." The serpent-tails recall the Eden serpent and anticipate the serpent-dragon of Revelation 12. The power of the horse-cavalry is both offensive (mouths) and dangerous from behind (tails) — a military formation impossible in normal warfare, signaling the supernatural character of this judgment-force.
20: ou metenoēsan — "they did not repent" (aorist negated). The purpose of the plagues is repentance; the repeated failure to repent (metanoeō appears in vv20-21 twice, and in Revelation 16:9,11) is the defining response of "those who dwell on the earth" (the world-system) to divine judgment. ta erga tōn cheirōn autōn (the works of their hands) — the idols; Psalms 115:4-7 and Revelation 135:15-17 describe idols as "the work of human hands" that cannot see, hear, or walk — the same three disabilities listed here.
21: phonōn ... pharmakōn ... porneias ... klemmaton — murders, sorceries, sexual immorality, thefts. Pharmakōn (sorceries) is from pharmakon (drug/poison/charm) — the word that gives English "pharmacy"; in the NT it consistently refers to occult practices that involve substances, spells, or enchantments (Galatians 5:20; Revelation 18:23; Revelation 21:8; Revelation 22:15). The fourfold list is a compressed moral inventory of the world-system's characteristic sins.
